coredata.dc:description: Zinc-ion electrochromic devices (ZECDs) represent an emerging technology with significant potential for energy-saving applications and smart electronics. Nevertheless, limited electrochemical kinetics, short cycle life and single color modulation prevent their further applications. Herein, by introducing carbon dots (CDs) into ZnCl2 water-in-salt (WIS) electrolyte, a stable and multicolor ZECD has been developed. According to experimental exploration and theoretical simulations, the WIS electrolyte demonstrates enhanced anion-cation interactions and decreased water activity, effectively suppressing the electrolysis of water molecules and significantly improving the overall performance of the device. Due to the complementary synergistic effect of electrodes and electrolytes, the obtained ZECDs exhibit high transmittance modulation (60.6 %), low driving voltage (1.5 V) and long cycle stability (around 92 % capacity retention after 1000 cycles). Significantly, the prototype Zn/ZnCl2@CDs/WO3 electrochromic display could present vivid colors and various fantastic patterns in 2D regional space. As a proof of concept, a novel temperature visualization sensing system was designed to monitor temperature signals from objects spanning various ranges, enabling clear display and immediate tracking of temperature variations. This work not only expands the applications of ZECDs in intelligent building systems but also paves the way for energy conservation, emission reduction and sustainable development.

head.ce:title: Stable and multicolor Zn-ion electrochromic devices with water-in-salt electrolyte toward intelligent visual sensing system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc-ion electrochromic devices (ZECDs) represent an emerging technology with significant potential for energy-saving applications and smart electronics. Nevertheless, limited electrochemical kinetics, short cycle life and single color modulation prevent their further applications. Herein, by introducing carbon dots (CDs) into ZnCl2 water-in-salt (WIS) electrolyte, a stable and multicolor ZECD has been developed. According to experimental exploration and theoretical simulations, the WIS electrolyte demonstrates enhanced anion-cation interactions and decreased water activity, effectively suppressing the electrolysis of water molecules and significantly improving the overall performance of the device. Due to the complementary synergistic effect of electrodes and electrolytes, the obtained ZECDs exhibit high transmittance modulation (60.6 %), low driving voltage (1.5 V) and long cycle stability (around 92 % capacity retention after 1000 cycles). Significantly, the prototype Zn/ZnCl2@CDs/WO3 electrochromic display could present vivid colors and various fantastic patterns in 2D regional space. As a proof of concept, a novel temperature visualization sensing system was designed to monitor temperature signals from objects spanning various ranges, enabling clear display and immediate tracking of temperature variations. This work not only expands the applications of ZECDs in intelligent building systems but also paves the way for energy conservation, emission reduction and sustainable development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demand of consumers for visual interaction, the development of interactive devices based on various visual feedback functions (such as luminescence, transparency, and color) has attracted considerable attention [1–5]. Generally, color as a vivid and attractive information, can provide a facile and efficient method for information transmission [6,7]. To date, inspired by the color-shifting function of animals in nature, various bio-inspired devices have been applied to sensor systems with color-changing capability [8–10]. Electrochromic devices (ECDs) are capable of responding to varying voltage stimuli and can reversibly change their color or transparency, making them widely applicable in information detection sensor systems. Integrating sensors with ECDs offers new possibilities for human–machine interaction due to high stability, fast response and low power consumption advantages. At present, some smart sensing systems based on different systems of ECDs (WO3//ITO, WO3//PB, WO3//PANI, etc.) were successfully constructed and applied to detect signals such as pressure, temperature and humidity [11–13]. While ECDs based on monovalent cations electrolytes (such as H+ and Li+) have demonstrated attractive cycling stability and rate capability, the limited reserves and the strong corrosion greatly hinder their real-world applications in intelligent visual sensing systems [14,15]. Therefore, developing safe electrolytes and corresponding reliable active materials is regarded as a paradigm shift within the electrochromic community.

Compared to monovalent cations (H+, Li+, Na+ and K+), multivalent-ion carriers (Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+ and Al3+) can can provide ECD with higher optical contrast, energy-recycling capability, and attractive long-term stability because of their multiple-electron transfer redox [2,16]. Among these multivalent metal ions, Zn-ions are particularly attractive due to the high theoretical capacity (820 mAh. g−1 and 5855 mAh. cm−3), relatively low redox potential (−0.76 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode) of Zn in mild aqueous electrolytes, chemical stability, natural abundance and environmental friendliness [17]. Consequently, Zn-based electrochromic devices (ZECDs) are considered one of the most promising contenders for next-generation high-performance visual sensing systems. However, factors such as the large radius of hydrated zinc ions and the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) hinder the kinetics of electrochromic reactions, leading to traps of zinc ions in electrochromic films and further destabilizing the performance of electrodes and devices. Therefore, the research on ZECD is still in the preliminary exploration period [2]. Recently, Zn2+-based water-in-salt (WIS) electrolytes provided a new pathway to overcome the abovementioned problems through reduced water activity and enhanced cation–anion interactions [18]. The high-concentrated ZnCl2 WIS electrolytes have been successfully applied in the ZECDs with satisfactory stability and enhanced electrochromic performance [19,20]. Nevertheless, most electrochromic materials always present relatively monotonous color changes during ZnCl2 WIS electrolytes, such as tungsten oxide (WO3) can only show a 1-fold color modulation between transparent and blue in previous studies [21,22]. Despite the vigorous progress in electrochromic materials and devices, fabricating ZECDs based on WIS electrolytes with multicolor display and developing modern multifunctional intelligent sensing systems based on ZECDs to broaden their practical applications remains a considerable challenge.

Currently, extensive studies have been launched into fabricating multicolor ZECDs based on inorganic electrochromic materials (such as WO3) by intrinsic color, structural color, and stacked color [23–27]. Therein, color superposition seems to be an efficient strategy to achieve multicolor of ZECDs [28,29]. Encouragingly, the stacked structure of ZECDs establishes a good foundation for color superposition and enabling it to endows each layer with different colors. However, giving the electrolyte layer an appropriate color remains a big challenge [30–32]. In this regard, carbon dots (CDs) with ultrasmall size (less than 10 nm) and multitudinous surface functional groups often could be dispersed homogeneously in various electrolytes [33–35]. Therefore, combining the of electrochromic electrodes with CDs-based electrolytes to design multicolor ZECDs is expected to result in acceptable visual aesthetics, which enables sensors to monitor and display multiple signals.

Herein, we systematically explore the performance of tungsten oxide electrochromic electrodes during ZnCl2 WIS electrolytes and demonstrate an efficient strategy to build a stable and multicolor ZECD. Combining experimental and theoretical simulation results, the electrochromic electrodes and devices presented a remarkable holistic performance at 7.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte, which is superior to that during other concentrations. Benefiting from employing CDs as electrolyte additives, the prototype Zn/ZnCl2@CDs/WO3 electrochromic display could present vivid colors and various fantastic patterns in 2D regional space. As a proof of concept, a novel temperature visualization sensing system was designed to serve as an effective dynamic visual feedback tool for intelligent building home. Encouragingly, the innovative visualization sensing system are playing an even more outstanding role in temperature monitoring and feedback in the realm of home safety, demonstrating significant potential in the next generation of intelligent building applications.

Initially, a transparent ITO substrate is chosen as the conductive base. The ITO glass is then cut into small samples measuring 2 × 5 cm2. Subsequently, the samples were sequentially immersed in anhydrous ethanol and deionized water, and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 mins each, followed by drying operation. Finally, the ITO undergoes dust removal treatment using a high-pressure ion air gun to complete the pre-treatment before sputtering. A WO3 electrochromic film is then deposited on the ITO glass using a magnetron sputtering method. These ITO-coated substrates with a sheet resistance of 25 Ω cm−2 were successively ultrasonically cleaned in anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 20 mins, and then one 10 mm edge of them was masked with vacuum tape as working electrode after dried in ambient air. The corresponding target with the diameter of 100 mm was pre-sputtered for 10 mins in order to prevent cross-contaminants prior to the deposition of every single layer. In the processes of preparation, the holder was rotated at a certain speed and the vertical distances between targets and substrate are approximately 150 mm. The vacuum chamber was initially pumped to a base pressure of 3.0 × 10−3 Pa. The flow rates of Ar (99.99 %) and O2 (99.99 %) were adjusted individually by mass flow controllers.

Firstly, 102 g ZnCl2 was added to 100 mL DI water solution. Subsequently, the prepared solution was stirred at room temperature for 10 mins. After that, a transparent electrolyte containing 7.5 M ZnCl2 was obtained. Simultaneously, cut the zinc sheet into a 3.5 × 3.5 cm2 sample, then cut a 2 × 2 cm2 small hole in the middle and paste it onto ITO glass. Subsequently, stick various parts together with foam double-sided adhesive tape to form a structure of ITO glass/WO3/Zn/ITO glass. Finally, the prepared electrolyte solution was injected between WO3 and ITO glass to successfully prepare an ITO glass/WO3/ZnCl2/Zn/ITO glass system ZECD.

The surface morphologies and microstructures of the sample were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Tescan, S8000) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM,JEOL-2100). The composition, electronic structure, and chemical bonds of the prepared samples were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI- 5400), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Vertex 70), and Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR-800514 nm).

The electrochemical characterization of WO3 and ZECDs was measured by the electrochemical workstation, such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), chronopotentiometry (CP) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD). For the WO3 electrode, its electrochemical performance was measured by a three-electrode system (WO3 film as the working electrode, Pt electrode as the counter electrode, and reference electrode as saturated Ag/AgCl) in ZnCl2 solution. Different from the WO3 electrode, the electrochemical performance of the ZECD was measured by two electrodes, with the WO3 as the working electrode and Zn as the counter and reference electrode. To further investigate the electrochromic performance of individual electrodes and ZECDs, the transmission spectrum was measured by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (PUXI, TU1901). The electrical characteristics of the conditioning circuit are measured by a high and low temperature test chamber and a digital multimeter. The high and low temperature test chamber provides the required temperature environment for the experiment, and a multi-functional digital multimeter is responsible for collecting data. The properties of the intelligent temperature visual sensing system were measured by a combination of high-low temperature test chambers and the UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The electrolyte is vital in the construction of ZECDs, influencing performance and ensuring efficient experimentation and goal attainment. Therefore, we first design and execute a series of comparative experiments aimed at precisely defining and analyzing the optimal concentration of ZnCl2 electrolyte for enhancing electrochemical performance, as well as exploring its potential mechanisms of action. ZnCl2 is known for its excellent solubility in water (Fig. S1a, Supporting Information) [19]. The ZnCl2 aqueous solutions at 5 M, 7.5 M, and 10 M display a high degree of transparency, whereas the solutions at 2 M and 15 M exhibit notable turbidity due to the formation of particles or aggregates resulting from solubility properties and intermolecular interactions. The effect of concentration on the optical properties of ZnCl2 solution was further quantified through spectral transmittance measurement (Fig. S1b, Supporting Information). ZnCl2 aqueous solutions with concentrations of 5 M, 7.5 M and 10 M exhibit high transmittance from 300 nm to 850 nm. On the contrary, 2 M and 15 M exhibit significant masking effects over a wide spectral range, which cannot meet the requirements for solution transparency in the experiment.

Additionally, Fig. 1
a provides a detailed description of the changes in conductivity and viscosity of ZnCl2 solutions with concentrations of 2 M, 5 M, 7.5 M, 10 M and 15 M, respectively. The results indicate that as the concentration of the solution increases, the conductivity of the ZnCl2 solution decreases. This phenomenon can be attributed to the significant ion pairing effect and restricted ion migration in high-concentration electrolyte solutions. Similarly, the viscosity of the solution gradually increases [2]. Moreover, Raman spectroscopy technology is specifically used to identify and analyze the molecular structure and the chemical composition of ZnCl2 solutions to explore their differences more deeply. A main peak located at approximately 286 cm−1 and a side peak located at approximately 390 cm−1 were observed in all concentrations of ZnCl2 solution (Fig. S2a, Supporting Information). To identify more accurately, we conducted peak fitting analysis and specifically selected 7.5 M and 15 M ZnCl2 solutions as representative samples of medium and high concentration ZnCl2 solutions for exploration (Fig. 1 b). In a 7.5 M ZnCl2 solution, three characteristic peaks were identified at 285.1 cm−1, 312.0 cm−1 and 404.6 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of Zn(H2O)2Cl4 2-, ZnCl+ and Zn(H2O)6 2+, respectively. When the concentration of ZnCl2 solution increases to 15 M, its internal components undergo significant changes. Specifically, the Raman characteristic peaks located at 236.5 cm−1 and 334.0 cm−1 respectively indicate the generation of ZnCl4 2- ions and Zn-Cl multinucleated aggregates. Meanwhile, under such high concentration conditions, the Raman peaks of Zn(H2O)6 2+ and ZnCl+ were replaced by the characteristic peaks of ZnCl4 2- and Zn-Cl multinucleated aggregates [36].

Furthermore, this study also utilized FTIR to verify the vibrational characteristics of molecules (Fig. S2b, Supporting Information). As the concentration of ZnCl2 gradually increases from 2 M to 15 M, the H-O–H bending vibration mode shows a trend towards lower wave numbers, with its wave value decreasing from 1625 cm−1 to 1612 cm−1. Meanwhile, in the spectral region of O–H stretching vibration, the symmetric stretching vibration peak located at 3200 cm−1 shows a weakened characteristic, while the asymmetric stretching vibration peak at 3400 cm−1 is relatively enhanced. This series of changes in vibration characteristics reveals a significant interaction force between water molecules and Zn2+ ions, which leads to the reconstruction and disorder of the O–H hydrogen bond network structure, thereby affecting the overall distribution of vibration characteristics [18]. To further investigate the intermolecular dynamics involving cations, anions, and water, as well as the hydration patterns of Zn2+ ions across varying electrolyte concentrations, comprehensive molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were conducted. Fig. 1 c-d present snapshots that illustrate the solvation sheaths and their surrounding chemical environments in 2 M and 7.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte solutions, respectively. In both electrolyte systems, Zn2+ ions were observed to hydrate through coordination with Cl- anions and oxygen atoms from water molecules. However, there are significant differences in the molecular composition of their solvation shells. Consequently, an in-depth analysis of coordination numbers was carried out to examine the characteristics of solvation structures associated with various electrolytes, employing radial distribution functions for a meticulous understanding [37–39]. In a 2 M ZnCl2 electrolyte solution, the coordination number for Zn-Cl bonds is 1.241, which is significantly lower than the coordination number of Zn-Cl in a 7.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte solution (1.847). On the contrary, the coordination number of Zn-O(H2O) in a 2 M ZnCl2 electrolyte solution is as high as 4.759, significantly exceeding the coordination number of Zn-O in a 7.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte solution of 4.153. Moreover, the peak intensity variation trend shown in these two graphs is highly consistent with the change in coordination number size (Fig. 1 e-f). These observations suggest that an increase in the electrolyte concentration significantly enhances the role of Cl- anions in facilitating the solvation of Zn2+ ions [40,41].

Macroscopically, the molality of ZnCl2 aqueous solution as a function of the weight ratio of ZnCl2 to H2O was verified (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). On a microscopic level, the differences in ion transport mechanisms between low-concentration and high-concentration electrolyte systems were further visually demonstrated (Fig. 1 g-h). In the low-concentration ZnCl2 electrolyte system, Cl- anions construct a continuous network of negatively charged channels. The Zn2+ ions located inside these channels not only form stable ionic bonds with Cl- anions but also dynamically interact with water molecules. The strong electrostatic interaction between this negatively charged Cl- channel and positively charged hydrated zinc ions (Zn(H2O)n 2+) provides a powerful driving force for the efficient migration of hydrated zinc ions in the electrolyte system. Nevertheless, as the concentration of ZnCl2 electrolyte increases, the ratio between water molecules and Zn2+ ions decreases, which directly leads to a significant decrease in the concentration of [Zn(H2O)2Cl4]2- complex ions and induces the generation of more anhydrous ion groups [ZnCl4]2-. Simultaneously, the significant enhancement of the interaction force between Zn2+ and Cl- not only changes the dynamic equilibrium between ions but also triggers an unfavorable mass transfer process, namely a decrease in the migration efficiency of ions in the electrolyte. This profoundly reveals the complexity and challenges of ion transport mechanisms in high-concentration electrolyte systems, in stark contrast to the efficient transport characteristics of ions in low-concentration environments [39]. Accordingly, after conducting an in-depth analysis of the characteristics of ZnCl2 solutions and considering subsequent experimental necessities, 5 M, 7.5 M and 10 M were selected as key concentration points to conduct in-depth research on the performance of WO3 thin films.

As a key component of the ZECDs, the quality of WO3 displays a direct and significant impact on various performance parameters of ZECDs. Therefore, it is essential to characterize the morphology, electrochemical, and electrochromic properties of the WO3 to ensure optimal device performance. As illustrated in Fig. 2
a, the WO3 electrode features a uniform distribution of porous nanostructures, which obviously not only provide a larger active surface area for the reaction but also shorten the ion transport path in the electrochemical process. Therefore, the porous structure of WO3 is very favorable for electrochemical reactions [42,43]. The sectional-view SEM image (inset in Fig. 2 a) shows that the WO3 film with a thickness of about 593 nm is firmly adhered to the ITO substrate, which underscores the robust construction and the stability of the interface. Meanwhile, the elemental mapping images reveal that W and O elements are uniformly distributed on the surface of the ITO electrode, indicating that the WO3 thin layer is successfully and uniformly coated on the ITO surface. Fig. 2 b provides a clear and typical TEM image of the WO3 thin film, revealing a meticulously uniform distribution of nanoclusters throughout its structure. This observation serves as an additional testament to the successful formation of the WO3 material, affirming its characteristic nanoscale morphology and composition [44].

Subsequently, the electrochemical and electrochromic properties of WO3 were evaluated. Schematic about the in-situ measurement of devices is provided as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). The optical contrast characteristics of WO3 thin films in different concentrations of ZnCl2 solutions within the spectral range of 300 nm to 850 nm were recorded (Fig. 2 c). Under constant and consistent coloring voltage (−0.9 V) and fading voltage (0.6 V) operating conditions, WO3 thin films display distinctly different electrochromic properties. Although the transmittance of WO3 films in different concentrations of solutions is almost the same in the colored state, their transmittance shows significant differentiation in the faded state, forming a sharp visual contrast. Specifically, in an electrolyte solution with a concentration of 7.5 M, the optical contrast difference between the colored and faded states of the WO3 film reaches its maximum value. Fig. 2 d provides a more detailed quantitative analysis of the effect of ZnCl2 solution concentration changes on the optical transmittance characteristics of WO3. When the concentration of ZnCl2 solution is precisely set to 7.5 M, the electrochromic modulation amplitude of WO3 reaches its maximum value, manifested as a optical modulation (ΔT) up to 55.4 %, ranging from 7 % in the colored state to 62.4 % in the bleached state. This value shows a significant increase of over 30 % compared to ΔT (20.7 %, from 4 % to 24.7 % at various states) in a 5 M ZnCl2 solution environment. In addition, high-resolution XPS spectra of WO3 negative electrode in colored/bleached states were recorded to explore key information such as the chemical composition and elemental valence states of WO3 in different states (Fig. S5, Supporting Information).

Additionally, long-term cycling performance plays a crucial role in evaluating the service life of electrode materials during experiments. Fig. 2 e presents the cyclic stability performance of the WO3 single electrode in ZnCl2 solutions with concentrations of 5 M, 7.5 M and 10 M. Significantly, the cycling performance of WO3 single electrode is significantly limited in ZnCl2 solutions with concentrations of 5 M and 10 M. Especially at a concentration of 10 M, the transmittance will significantly decrease after a short cycle period. Conversely, WO3 exhibits excellent cycling stability in a 7.5 M concentration ZnCl2 solution. After the same number of cycles, its ΔT remains at a high level, with only a minimal decrease compared to the initial state. Fig. S6 (Supporting Information) thoroughly reveals the excellent color control performance and cycling stability characteristics exhibited by the 7.5 M ZnCl2 solution in the study, further verifying its significant advantages at this concentration.

Besides, this study also delved into the electrochemical behavior characteristics of WO3 electrodes in electrolyte systems with different concentrations of ZnCl2 solution. Fig. 2 f provides a detailed study of the CV characteristics of WO3 in 7.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte at different scan speeds. The results show that as the scan speed increases, the peak significantly widens, indicating the inherent intercalation pseudocapacitive behavior of WO3 [45]. Further quantitative analysis shows that at a low scan rate of 5 mV/s, the diffusion control process contributes approximately 75 % to the total capacitance, highlighting its dominant position (Fig. 2 g). As the scanning rate increases, the importance of surface capacitance gradually increases, becoming a key factor affecting performance (Fig. 2 h). This trend is similar to the reaction kinetics of battery-type electrodes, attributed to incomplete redox reactions driven by ion diffusion under high-speed scanning [15,46]. The CV curves and capacitance contribution changes of WO3 electrodes in the ZnCl2 electrolytes of other concentrations were also presented in Fig. S7-S10 (Supporting Information). Comparative analysis reveals that although the diffusion control contribution increases with scanning rate, its overall proportion decreases with increasing solution concentration, and CV curves above 7.5 M concentration tend to be disordered. Furthermore, additional electrochemical properties of the WO3 single electrode weresubjected to testing in Fig. S11-S12 (Supporting Information).

Subsequently, electrochromic devices are fabricated, with the aim of exhibiting the utilization of WO3 thin films in conjunction with ZnCl2 WIS electrolytes. The manufacturing process and the entire ZECD structure are shown in Fig. 3
a. As described in detail in the experimental process, the prepared ZnCl2 electrolyte solution was injected into the structure of ITO glass/WO3/Zn/ITO glass through a syringe, and the ITO glass/WO3/ZnCl2/Zn/ITO glass system ZECD was successfully prepared [47]. Meanwhile, quantitative measurement of the ZECD was conducted using a UV–visible spectrometer to investigate its electrochromic performance [8,48]. Fig. 3 b illustrates the transmittance spectra within the wavelength range of 300 nm to 900 nm, as recorded under various applied voltages. Crucially, the ZECD will also undergo a reversible color change during its work process [49]. When at a charge state, the ZECD shows a transparent color and is accompanied by high visible light transmittance. As the applied voltage increases, the transmittance of the ZECD will increase accordingly. Apparently, the transmittance of the ZECD decreases from 62.3 % to 1.7 % with voltage variation at 630 nm, achieving approximately 60.6 % ΔT. Moreover, the coloring/bleaching switching time of the ZECD at this wavelength was determined to be 7.5 s and 4.5 s, respectively, indicating that the ZECD could provide a timely color response to temperature changes (Fig. 3 c). Furthermore, the resultant ZECD presents a variety of color states, ranging from transparent at 0.1 V to a dark blue shade at 1.5 V (Fig. 3 d and Video S1, Supporting Information). The aforementioned research meticulously demonstrates that ZECDs exhibit remarkable color-controllable characteristics, which can be effortlessly monitored and observed by the naked eye without the aid of sophisticated instruments or equipment.

Apart from the level of color change, the stability of the ZECD also plays an indispensable role in terms of temperature monitoring. Fig. 3 e illustrates the transmittance of in-situ coloring and bleaching during the electrochemical reaction of ZECDs, which varies with the voltage cycle. After 1000 voltage cycles, the optical modulation capability of ZECD can still be maintained at 92 % of its initial value, fully demonstrating its excellent stability and reliability during long-term cyclic use. The aforementioned study suggests that the ZECD demonstrates superior electrochromic characteristics. Fig. 3 f-g displays a schematic diagram of the structural response of ZECD, which is composed of positive materials Zn, negative material WO3 and liquid electrolyte ZnCl2. Zn-ions are extracted from the Zn electrode and intercalated into the WO3 electrode during the charging process. Meanwhile, the electrons moved in the contrary direction by the external circuit, leading to the WO3 electrode of the ZECD exhibiting a colored state. Similarly, during the discharge process, the electrons and Zn-ions will move in the contrary direction and the ZECD shows a bleached state [15,44].

Research has shown that ZECD performs well as the display part of intelligent temperature sensing systems, but further understanding of its regulating circuit and analysis of its electrical characteristics are needed to improve system applications [6,50]. Therefore, detailed testing and analysis were conducted on the performance of the conditioning circuit. Distinct from conventional sensing circuits, positioning the temperature sensor at the feedback terminal of the operational amplifier enables the direct attainment of a reverse voltage output, thereby eliminating the necessity for an external reverse voltage to reset the color state of the ZECD (Fig. 4
a). The conditioning circuit consists of a camera module, a power supply module, a temperature input module, and a voltage output module [51,52]. More details about the conditioning circuit are shown in Fig. S13 (Supporting Information).

A rigorous testing procedure was used to evaluate the performance of the regulating circuit, placing the circuit in a temperature gradient gradually increasing from 0 ℃ to 100 ℃. During this process, the dynamic response of the circuit output voltage to changes in ambient temperature was accurately recorded. Specifically, as the ambient temperature gradually increases, the output voltage of the circuit changes significantly from the initial value of 0 V until reaching a peak of 1.35 V (Fig. 4 b). There is an inherent correlation between the voltage signal generated by the circuit and the applied temperature within specific temperature ranges of 20−40 ℃, 20−60 ℃, 20−80 ℃ and 20−100 ℃ (Fig. 4 c). With the periodic change of temperature, the voltage also shows a responsive change and exhibits stable repetitive performance. Meanwhile, the detail of the response time of the conditioning circuit at various temperature fluctuations is also investigated (Fig. 4 d). Notably, as the voltage range expands from 20−40 ℃ to 20−100 ℃, the time for voltage rise is 12 s, 9 s, 15 s, and 14 s, respectively. Meanwhile, the time of voltage drop gradually shows an extended trend as the voltage range expands, which are 20 s, 18 s, 12 s and 10 s, respectively. The test outcomes are in accordance with the fundamental circuit laws, attesting to the robust linearity and swift responsiveness of the regulating circuit. Additionally, the conditioning circuit is also eager to exhibit outstanding stability behavior at various temperatures. Fig. 4 e reveals the results of stability tests performed on the regulating circuit across various temperatures, including 40 ℃, 60 ℃, 80 ℃ and 100 ℃. Noteworthy, the output voltage of the conditioning circuit remains constant even after being kept at various temperatures for 1 h, which is well consistent with the volt-temperature result. This further validates the regulating circuit's advantages of rapid response and exceptional stability, establishing a solid foundation for the realization of temperature visualization monitoring.

Generally speaking, the outstanding performance of ZECDs and conditioning circuits is a prerequisite for constructing a temperature visualization sensing system. Subsequently, a temperature visualization sensing system was constructed by integrating the conditioning circuit, ZECDs, and temperature sensors to visually monitor signals [53,54]. As the core sensing component of the system, the PT100 temperature sensor is responsible for real-time monitoring of temperature changes in objects and converting them into corresponding changes in resistance values.Simultaneously, the temperature regulation circuit accurately converts the resistance change of the sensor into a voltage signal, ensuring that the signal perfectly matches the color change range of ZECD.After receiving these voltage signals, ZECD will undergo reversible color changes, intuitively reflecting the temperature state of the object [55–57]. Eventually, the monitoring module records the color changes of ZECDs and sends real-time temperature data to smart devices, making it convenient for users to check the temperature at any time (Fig. 4 f).

To specifically investigate the properties of the intelligent temperature visual sensing system, a test platform that consisted of a temperature measurement module, monitor module, power supply module, display module, upper computer and high-low temperature test chamber was built. The temperature-sensing module is in charge of collecting diverse temperature signals and transforming them into resistance signals. The circuit module, including the monitor module, power supply module, and conditioning module, is responsible for the voltage required to convert the resistance signal into the color change of a ZECD.

The color of the display module also undergoes stable and reversible changes due to changes in applied voltage, which will be displayed on the upper computer (Fig. 5
a-b). The upper computer interface presents a specific set of colors, each corresponding to different temperatures: 100 ℃ and 40 ℃ (Fig. 5 c). These color representations are in harmony with the visuals depicted in Video S2. Considerably, the prepared ZECD displays multi-color properties and exhibits different colors with temperature changes. Specifically, when the temperature drops from 100 ℃ to 80 ℃, the ZECD changes from a transparent state to a light blue color, then gradually deepens at 60 ℃, and finally turns into a deep blue color at 40 ℃ (Fig. 5 d). This finding suggests not only that the color change of the ZECD is highly controllable but also validates its potential as a display module capable of achieving real-time monitoring of the device's operational status with precision and efficiency [58].

In order to gain a deeper understanding of the optical performance of ZECDs at different temperatures, the visible light transmittance variation of the display module was recorded as a function of temperature in the wavelength range of 300 nm to 900 nm (Fig. 5 e). Apparently, the maximum transmittance modulation calculated at 630 nm is 44.3 %. Moreover, the transmittance shows significant differences at 40 ℃, 60 ℃, 80 ℃ and 100 ℃, respectively, indicating that the intelligent temperature visual sensing system can accurately achieve visual monitoring of temperature. Besides good accuracy performance, the response speed and stability performance have also been investigated during continuous temperature changes. Fig. 5 f shows the reversibility of the display module at a wavelength of 630 nm between thetemperature range of 20−100 ℃, 20−80 ℃, 20−60 ℃ and 20−40 ℃, respectively. The coloration of the display module was determined to be 18 s, 22 s, 34 s, 39 s and the bleaching processes were 11 s, 13 s, 27 s, 30 s, comparable to those of other temperature sensing systems in previous reports. Additionally, the stability of the system is closely bound up with the experience of users. Hence, Fig. 5 g illustrates the stability of the intelligent temperature visual sensing system at temperatures of 40 ℃, 60 ℃, 80 ℃ and 100 ℃. When the external temperature signals remain stable and unchanged, the transmittance of the display module is basically fixed, indicating that an intelligent temperature visual sensing system possesses outstanding stability. The results of the research underscore the impressive qualities of the temperature sensor system, including its precision in measurements, rapid responsiveness, and remarkable stability.

Despite the effectiveness of real-time visualization technology for temperature monitoring, the demand for temperature control in modern households is becoming increasingly complex and diverse. Due to the large number of household appliances, a system that only monitors a single temperature point is no longer sufficient, especially when multiple areas need to be monitored simultaneously. Therefore, based on previous research, an innovative electrochromic multi-channel temperature visualization monitoring system is introduced, which can accurately monitor multiple temperature sources in parallel, improving the efficiency and flexibility of home temperature management (Fig. 6
a). Yet, inorganic ZECDs with simple structures frequently showcase relatively monotonous colors, resulting in challenges like the indistinct differentiation of multiple signals [59,60]. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced into the electrolyte layer of ZECD for color overlay, expanding the color range of ZECD to meet the need for simultaneous visual monitoring of multiple temperature signals. The preparation process of multi-color electrolytes is shown in the (Fig. S14, Supporting Information). The structural composition and morphology of the prepared CDs was characterized by Raman, FTIR, XPS and TEM (Fig. S15-S17, Supporting Information).

Clearly, CDs exhibit uniformly dispersed spherical shapes with an average size of approximately 3.8 nm and has well-resolved lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. Therefore, CDs can usually be considered as composed of graphite like carbon cores and a large number of amorphous structures with rich functional groups on the surface, which lays the physical foundation for the superposition of CDs color and electrochromic layer color [61,62]. Benefiting from the complementary synergistic effect of electrochromic electrode materials and CDs, tthe specific color fluorescence emitted by CDs interweaves with the color presented by the electrochromic layer, resulting in rich color changes in the newly prepared ZECD (Fig. S18, Supporting Information) [63,64].

The rich color variations displayed by ZECD ingeniously reflect its infinite potential in the field of smart homes. It allows the system to deploy multiple sensors on household appliances such as refrigerators, fish tanks, and electric heaters, displaying real-time temperature monitoring of different temperature ranges through unique colors, visualizing temperature data, and helping users easily grasp the temperature dynamics in their homes. Fig. 6 b shows a more detailed display ofthe operating mechanism of the system, where the temperature sensor serves as the core component to collect external temperature signals and convert them into electrical signals. On the one hand, the STM32 control module receives and processes electrical signals from temperature sensors through built-in specific algorithms and program logic, calculates and outputs accurate voltage signals that can drive the color change of ZECD, and thereby makes ZECD appear in different colors, thereby realizing the function of sensing temperature range through color. For example, when a lower temperature is detected, the voltage signal output after STM32 processing will cause ZECD to exhibit a specific cool tone; As the temperature increases, the output voltage signal changes, and ZECD gradually transitions to a warm color tone, intuitively reflecting the change in temperature range through color changes. On the other hand, by communicating with the intelligent machine, the color signals of the ZECDs are transmitted to the intelligent machines for visual monitoring through the camera. The whole process includes the collection, processing, transmission, and monitoring of temperature information, reflecting the integrity and feasibility of the intelligent sensing system.

Fig. 6 c-e vividly demonstrate the accuracy and intuitiveness of the intelligent temperature visual sensing system in monitoring temperature changes in refrigerators, fish tanks, and electric heaters. For the specific application scenario of refrigerators, the system is carefully designed to operate within the ideal preservation range of −20 ℃ to 10 ℃, ensuring that the freshness and long-term freezing effect of food are best maintained. When the refrigerator is maintained at its optimal temperature, the indicator declares its stable state in blue; as the temperature rises, the blue gradually fades away and transforms into a pale yellow tone, intuitively mapping the trajectory of temperature changes. Furthermore, to enhance the recognition and accuracy of the system in different application scenarios, CDs are cleverly integrated into the ZECDs display module for temperature-sensing systems of fish tanks and electric heaters. The fish tank area exhibits blue-green color at low temperatures, which is the result of the perfect fusion of carbon dots and electrode colors. As the water temperature gradually rises, the equipment transitions to a brown bleached state, and this color evolution process is thoroughly recorded in the system's chromatogram (Fig. 6 d). As for the electric heater, its temperature changes are vividly displayed through the gradient color from dark green to orange-red on the ZECD display module (Fig. 6 e). This series of color conversions not only accurately reflects the temperature fluctuations during the operation of the electric heater, but also enhances users' perception and understanding of temperature changes with visual impact. In summary, the intelligent temperature visual sensing system has successfully achieved real-time feedback and accurate presentation of temperature changes with its keen color perception and changeability, bringing users an unprecedented intelligent living experience.

In summary, we have successfully developed a stable and multicolor ZECD utilizing 7.5 M ZnCl2 water-in-salt (WIS) electrolytes. The effectiveness of the ZnCl2 WIS electrolyte in reducing water activity and significantly enhancing cation–anion interactions contributes to the exceptional performance of the ZECDs. Benefiting from the complementary synergistic effect of electrodes and electrolytes, the prototype ZECD exhibits a remarkable transmittance modulation capability of 60.6 %, operates at a low driving voltage of 1.5 V, boasts rapid response times (7.5 s) and demonstrates outstanding cycling stability (maintaining 92 % of the initial state after 1000 cycles). By employing CDs as electrolyte additives, the color transition of the resultant ZECDs from a one-dimensional change from transparent to deep blue to a two-dimensional multi-color superposition, significantly boosts their electrochromic performance. As a proof of concept, the design of a new temperature visualization sensing system that integrates ZECDs and temperature sensors can reflect different temperature ranges with unique colors, achieve efficient monitoring of segmented temperatures, and provide an effective dynamic visual feedback method for the fields of smart buildings and homes. This advancement not only elevates temperature sensing technology but also opens up new avenues for the application of electrochromic technology in smart environments.
